title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee approved several items including awarding a $126,900 asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, directing staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100/day, and recommending construction service contracts for two road projects. The committee also acknowledged receipt of a $750,000+ state grant for Birch Street reconstruction.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 Water and Sewer Permits
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC Annual Report
Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
Construction Service Proposals - Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel Projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Scofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally a 2029 project now likely moving to 2028. The grant covers over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion included Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for road depressions over sewer trenches, with the contractor understanding repairs may need to continue if settling recurs.

Staff reported road limits were removed April 29th along with Marathon County. Spring activities included mailbox repairs, sign repairs, curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Flooding issues required dewatering pumps along the Weston Avenue tributary for about a week. Staff discussed potential permanent pump solutions for recurring flooding in the Crane Meadows area. Jones Street culvert failures required installing a third 18-inch culvert. Equipment maintenance included removing plow equipment, servicing mowers, and preparing vehicles for summer operations.

Josh reported significant clear water infiltration into the sanitary sewer system. In April, the village pumped 37 million gallons into the water system but treated nearly 54 million gallons of wastewater - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. A Kathleen lift station example showed one home's sump pump was causing continuous pumping at a station normally cycling three times daily. The INI study with In-Pipe Technologies will help identify problem areas. The Carrie well rehab started today, and water main flushing notices are going out with flushing starting next week.

Michael presented the annual Public Service Commission report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with the prior year's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service including wells 7 and 8 and water mains from the Weston Avenue project. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers - 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in other areas. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supports long-term sustainable state transportation funding. He noted grant funding helps one-time costs but recurring operational funding for street maintenance and snow plowing requires general tax dollars or other local revenue sources. The village has decreased its public works budget recently. Wisconsin is relatively affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership costs. Motion to recommend village board adoption passed unanimously.

Michael presented the recycled base course crushing contract. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons, totaling $126,900. This was only $0.02/ton less than the next bidder. The village saves approximately $11 per cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, translating to about $130,000 savings on this year's three projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on enforcement of sewer discharge violations, particularly sump pump discharges into sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day. Josh noted the village treated nearly 15 million extra gallons in April due to infiltration. Committee member Jordan suggested distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. The committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines starting at $100/day. Staff will wait for the INI study results to identify problem areas before sending violation notices. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael explained a builder wants to construct a duplex on lot 1 of Radkey Avenue which lacks frontage on a water/sewer main. Staff proposes allowing a temporary long lateral service until Radkey Avenue reconstruction, when the owner would pay assessment costs. Committee requested adding a deadline - 'no later than 2029' for reconstruction. Motion to recommend approval with the 2029 deadline passed unanimously.

Michael presented an intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Rothschild holds the contract with Steam Construction. Weston's share is $94,860 for water main relocation ($88,704) and sanitary sewer ($6,156) work needed due to storm sewer conflicts. The water main will have 18 inches of cover with 8 inches of insulation. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented construction inspection proposals received Friday. For the Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project, MSA bid $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910; staff recommends MSA. For Bladel Avenue, MSA bid $70,400 using a summer intern, while Clark Dietz bid $73,730 with full-time staff; staff recommends Clark Dietz for the additional experience despite the slightly higher cost. Motion to approve MSA for the roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100/day
Recommend village board approve water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkey Avenue with 2029 reconstruction deadline
Recommend village board approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild ($94,860 Weston share)
Recommend village board approve MSA for Alderson/Jelnik construction services ($37,315) and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue ($73,730)
Village board to adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Special assessment meeting scheduled for Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM for Bladel Avenue project
Norcon to begin concrete repairs on Scofield Avenue eastbound lanes starting Wednesday/Thursday
Staff to provide estimate of costs associated with clear water infiltration at next meeting
Staff to investigate adding street lighting to Scofield Avenue from Birch Street to Camp Phillips Road as part of future reconstruction

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Work product transmittals, educational presentations and reports
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated for Vice Chair by one member, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary unanimously.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. The motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill (3308 Green Pastures Lane) represented the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year. He announced that a competing Marshfield hot air balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole event in the area. Commissioner Hodal asked about room usage and requested better tracking data from hotels to help future funding decisions. The commission noted the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year when they received $5,000. Motion to approve $5,000 grant passed unanimously, with the commission requesting detailed hotel room data in their post-event report.

Renee reported attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting that Anastasia won the title. The candidates visited various Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese. This was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since approximately the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow Balloonfest organizers to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with the commission addressing technical requirements and future expansion considerations.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Open public comment period for items not on agenda
Item reordering - moving item six before item five
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Future agenda items and staff updates

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive, which meets all B2 district zoning requirements. Police Chief Jeremy explained challenges with a gas pipeline on the property that required rotating the building orientation to face east and placing it on the higher northern portion of the site. The building has capacity for 50-60 officers, up from current staffing of 40 officers and 4 clerical staff. Discussion addressed an alternate storage shed location, future expansion possibilities to the north, and landscaping that meets current relaxed code standards. Motion to approve was made and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff proposed a twice-annual inspection program for salt storage facilities within wellhead protection areas - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and a winter operational inspection focusing on material handling practices. The program includes a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards. Staff noted one existing facility near the village well on Sternberg Road that predates the wellhead protection ordinance and has never gone through the conditional use process. Commission directed staff to reach out to that facility about compliance. Motion to approve with reporting to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Jennifer announced the first meeting of the joint planning commission and village board steering committee for the comprehensive plan update is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. She will send email confirmation and calendar invites to commissioners.

Electric vehicle charging stations will be a future agenda item, likely in the fall. Commissioner raised interest in residential TIF districts for workforce housing. Staff mentioned Ehlers has a presentation on residential TIF qualifications that could be shared with the commission, potentially as part of the comprehensive plan steering committee discussions.

publicComment: Jim Penson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He asked the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, urging staff to waive excessive requirements similar to what was done for the Greenheck Turner building. He also requested that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on site visits and compliance findings.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive approved unanimously with staff conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with twice-annual inspections
Staff to report back on inspection program findings to both Plan Commission and Public Works committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg Road regarding wellhead protection compliance
First comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email and calendar invites for steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging stations to be future agenda item in fall 2026

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an organizational meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Service Office, and Aging & Disability Resource Center. The committee approved minutes from April 1st and received comprehensive overviews of departmental missions, budgets, and key initiatives. No major policy decisions were made as this was primarily an informational session.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote participation confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - no items
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04 - missions, duties, responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Office presentation by VSO Aaron Galindo
Aging & Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli moved to approve the minutes. Vice Chair Rosenberg seconded. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting aye.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's structure under section 2.04 subsection 5, including relationships with Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, North Central Healthcare Board, and Board of Health. Chair Boots noted the committee will be developing a new strategic plan by December and will align meeting agendas with strategic plan objectives.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting from environmental hazards. The department has 37.6 FTE staff across four teams: environmental health (1,200+ licenses), nursing/communicable disease, community health improvement, and administration. Suginaka highlighted public health vending machines offering free tick kits and other resources, the Nurse Family Partnership program graduating its first two-year-olds, and a new homelessness system response position created from a task force last session.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa, explaining the department's approximately 140 FTE covering child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program done in partnership with LaCrosse and Wood counties, noting significant success working with families who had never had bank accounts or been on leases. The department's 2026 budget is over $22 million with about $7 million in tax levy.

VSO Aaron Galindo reported the office increased to 3.0 FTE this year. Customer interactions jumped from 5,900 to 8,149 in 2025. The PACT Act has significantly increased claims, with $44 million in annual VA benefits flowing to Marathon County veterans and $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Galindo emphasized priorities including surviving spouse benefits (up to $2,400/month tax-free), housing stability through Center for Veterans Issues partnership, and suicide prevention noting 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional collaboration; Galindo explained state statute requires each county have a VSO but veterans can work with any office statewide.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the four-county Aging & Disability Resource Center serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties with about 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers. The ADRC provides information assistance, caregiver support, dementia care, falls prevention, Medicare/Medicaid benefit specialists, and Meals on Wheels. Ray noted significant financial challenges with nutrition program funding flat at 2024 levels while food costs increased 40-45% over four years, requiring reduced meals, service days, and staffing. Marathon County's contribution is $413,135. Vice Chair Rosenberg thanked the program for culturally appropriate meals at the Hmong American Center.

actionItems: Minutes from April 1st meeting approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan information to upcoming meetings
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (not May 29th as initially stated)
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with agenda item suggestions

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increases to park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made appointments to the CLIPS committee.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call, pledge of allegiance
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Acknowledgment of committee reports and chair updates
Consent agenda including vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, and transportation funding resolution
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public Hearing on Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park impact fees from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment questioning whether the village has actual data on park maintenance costs. Trustee Barb supported the fee increase stating it's fair for new homes to pay for increased park needs. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell and second by Zagami. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling and second by Clark. Both passed with no opposition noted.

Chief Finkey explained this ordinance creates a regional mutual recognition system for food truck fire inspections. Participating municipalities including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and others will accept each other's inspection stickers, eliminating the need for multiple inspections. Motion by Lope, second by Zagami, passed unanimously.

The board discussed regulations for e-bikes and e-motos on sidewalks, with trustees noting e-motos can travel up to 52 mph and are different from e-bikes due to having engines rather than pedals. Motion by Zagami, second by Clark, passed unanimously.

Significant debate occurred over the policy language. Trustees agreed to strike sentences requiring specific health/safety/welfare justifications for remote attendance, leaving only requirements to notify 12 hours in advance, be on camera, and not participate in closed session. Trustees Hermling and Lope Sorrell voted against the amendment to strike the justification language. The amended policy passed with two opposed.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lope, passed unanimously. The board also agreed to include committee applicant resumes in future board packets.

The board discussed challenges with the new Kennedy Park location after losing the Camp Phillips/Schofield Avenue site to development. Trustee Lisa Beck raised safety concerns about parking on grass areas. Renee explained the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor spots with the moves. Discussion included whether Mock Miller Park would work (concerns about being too far from traffic) and whether the market should pause if no suitable location exists. The market generates revenue and Renee confirmed grants cover token programs and she is not losing money. No action taken; discussion only.

Director Michael Dolan reported on ongoing speed study efforts. The engineering study showed the 85th percentile speed was 45 mph, but construction has delayed completion of additional required data collection. A resident inquiry noted the speed is currently inconsistent with village ordinance. Staff will bring this back to the board.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street offered two public comments. During general public comment, he criticized hiring outside engineering consultants for $108,000 when an engineering technician position remains unfilled, questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced as a Safer board member before official appointment, and complained about lack of information on Kennedy Park in the packet. During the park impact fee hearing, he questioned whether the village has actual data on park maintenance costs to justify the fee increase.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Mobile food vendor regional inspection recognition system adopted via Ordinance 26-008
E-bike regulations adopted via Ordinance 26-012
Resolution 2026-014 supporting long-term transportation funding adopted
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license approved for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC contingent on background check and occupancy permit
Comprehensive Plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 pm
Staff to prepare intergovernmental agreement for Ringle building inspection assistance
Committee applicant resumes to be included in future board packets
Staff to bring Weston Avenue speed limit issue back to board after completing speed study

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board held an adjourned organizational meeting where committee appointments were approved, two at-large executive committee members were elected (Supervisors Hapa and Arstead), and a resolution was passed to reduce landfill tipping fees for debris from the recent severe weather emergency. The board also heard from residents affected by the tornado in Ringle requesting additional fee relief.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Committee appointments presentation by Chair Gibbs
Election of at-large executive committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diversity of perspective, developing new supervisors as leaders, and respecting supervisor preferences. The appointments included both County Board Chair and County Administrator appointments. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous. Supervisor Nisa noted the system marked him absent but didn't allow him to vote, clarifying he was an 'I' vote.

discussions.item: Election of at-large executive committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg, who cited her advocacy for children and elderly), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Supervisor Boots). Paper ballots were distributed for board members to select two. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on consent. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
discussions.body: Resolution from Health and Human Services and HR Finance and Property committees. Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for severe weather debris
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher presented the proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for disaster-related debris. The state is waiving $13.10, Ringle waiving $2.33, resulting in a proposed fee of $36.94 per ton covering landfill costs. Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck spoke requesting Marathon County wave fees entirely, noting 44 homes affected and insurance adjusters still pending. Multiple supervisors asked questions about coverage, timeline, and policy implications. Administrator Leonard mentioned potential partnership with Community Foundation for unreimbursed costs. The board suspended rules unanimously to consider the resolution, then passed Resolution 26-26 unanimously with motion by Supervisor Arstead and second by Supervisor Morash.

publicComment: Two residents were given special dispensation to speak on the tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty urged the county to wave fees entirely, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle are all waiving their portions, and requested relief for six months. She stated one business has spent $40,000 on cleanup and that the Community Foundation has received $180,000 in donations. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of affected homeowners, noting 44 homes affected with some insurance adjusters still not having arrived, requesting fee relief especially for underinsured residents.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to executive committee at-large positions
Reduced landfill tipping fees approved for disaster-related debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator to work with Community Foundation on mechanism for reimbursing unreimbursed cleanup costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop clear policy on handling similar emergency situations within six months
Staff to continue tracking disaster-related debris with certification forms
Board to revisit tipping fee timeline at May meeting if extension needed

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver proposal to help tornado victims, limiting the waiver to only septic system reconnect fees rather than the broader waiver originally proposed by staff. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) - Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (conservancy recreation to general agriculture) - Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for or against. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval. Supervisor Kurt seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from conservancy recreation to general agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature, and Pogle explained the property has a home and horse riding stable - the rezone makes the home a conforming use rather than non-conforming. No natural areas would be lost. Town of McMillan recommended approval April 20, 2026. No public testimony. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, Supervisor Drew seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather victims
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented staff recommendation to waive county zoning and septic fees for tornado-affected properties. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Leur asked about tracking costs and potential levy impacts. Administrator Lance Leonhard noted this is non-anticipated revenue and wouldn't require additional staff. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concerns about fairness to other disaster victims not in declared areas. Marilyn Bend asked about septic hookup costs. After discussion, Supervisor Sefeld moved to forward the resolution. Supervisor Drebeck amended the motion to only waive reconnect fees (not other zoning or pouts fees). Supervisor K seconded the amendment. Amendment passed. The amended motion to forward to county board passed, but not unanimously.

Director Dave Hagen Busher presented department operations including landfill management, renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, and upcoming leachate treatment facility requirements by 2032. He noted the landfill accepts about 1,000 tons daily with 150-200 transactions. Major capital projects include $6 million for 11-acre construction and $3-4 million closures. The clean water fund application was deemed incomplete and will be resubmitted for 2027 funding.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
discussions.body: County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie. The department has 44 full-time and 120 seasonal staff, operates 17 county parks, 30,700 acres of county forest, and provides services to City of Wausau for about 23-25 FTEs. A Nine Mile County Forest study showed out-of-town visitors generate $3.25 million for the county economy. Supervisor Sefeld noted Tom is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service, with Joe Tucker selected as his replacement.

actionItems: Rezoning for James Offer and David Tract property (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) in Town of Green Valley forwarded to County Board with approval recommendation
Rezoning for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz property (conservancy recreation to general agriculture) in Town of McMillan forwarded to County Board with approval recommendation
Modified fee waiver resolution (reconnect fees only) for April 17, 2026 severe weather victims forwarded to County Board
Staff to track costs and requests related to fee waivers for future policy consideration
Conservation Planning and Zoning to present conservation topics at a future summer meeting

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks and Recreation Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month, approved signage for Yellow Banks kayak launches along the Plover River, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and development. The meeting included public comment opposing the dog day event and criticism of Kennedy Park financial reporting.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on Oaklair River Trail discussion
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27th, 2026 meeting were approved. A motion was made, seconded, and passed unanimously with no discussion.

Staff presented a timeline of the Kennedy Park project from the comprehensive plan approval in November 2023 through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Director Sean Oster explained that Friends of Kennedy Park plans to announce fundraising totals at their June tournament with a check presentation to the community foundation. The group currently has approximately $84,000-$85,000 at the community foundation toward a $117,000 goal for field lights. Committee member Scott asked clarifying questions about village capital dollars, and staff confirmed no village funds are currently earmarked for Kennedy Park construction pending fundraising. The chair emphasized that master plans are guidance documents subject to change and that the volunteer Friends group deserves grace in their fundraising efforts.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff provided photos and a PDF showing the location of trees removed from the river and the installation of wood mats over gas pipelines to protect them during construction. Dan explained the mat installation process and showed equipment photos. Two gas pipeline crossings required protective mats, with extra material added for approaches.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to next month because Committee Chair Katrina was absent due to an unforeseen issue. The chair noted that since Katrina had requested this agenda item, she should have the opportunity to speak on it. One audience member (Andy) who spoke during public comment opposing dogs at the pool was told his objection was noted.

Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and benches on the trail, noting their pressure washer cannot access the trail due to bridge width limitations and steep ditches. Memorial benches weigh 2,000 pounds each. Committee members suggested applying anti-vandalism coatings or sealers, and discussed potential volunteer work days for maintenance. No formal action was taken; staff will research options.

Dan from Marathon County presented version seven of interpretive signs for Plover River kayak launches. Three signs will be installed in Weston (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks). The signs include maps, QR codes linking to the Kelly Station river gauge, and donation links to Marathon County Parks Foundation. The Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement. Motion to approve village installation of the signs passed unanimously.

Staff requested interest in discussing food allergy awareness signage at playgrounds after receiving a request from an individual offering to provide signs. Committee agreed to bring this back for future discussion along with the deferred dog day item. Staff also announced the Mock Miller Park master plan process kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for the following Tuesday.

publicComment: Two public comments were received. Andy (6504 [address inaudible]) spoke in person opposing dogs at the public pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston) spoke online criticizing the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioning Kennedy Park financial reporting, calling the $8,700 figure for baseball equipment 'too coincidental' and asking where the $10,000 grant money and Glow Games fundraiser proceeds are accounted for. He suggested the Friends of Kennedy Park may be 'frauds' for not sharing complete financials.
actionItems: Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Yellow Banks kayak launch signs approved for installation by village staff
Staff to research anti-vandalism coatings and cleaning options for Oaklair River Trail benches
Food allergy awareness playground signage to be brought back for future discussion
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Bike and pedestrian plan open house scheduled for June 11th, 5-7 p.m. in training room
Next meeting scheduled for June 22nd, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee held a routine meeting featuring budget reports and an educational presentation on the municipal clerk's office operations. Key discussions included TIF district bond payoff procedures scheduled for fall, election administration costs, and the farmers market's successful opening at Kennedy Park.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Staff remarks and farmers market update

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes from the previous Finance and HR meeting was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

A committee member asked about the timeline for final TIF bond payoff. Staff explained bonds are due October 1st and the CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, likely this summer. Staff confirmed the numbers still look good and they will be able to pay off the debt. The reports were acknowledged along with other work product transmittals.
Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report was made by Daniels and seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented an overview of the clerk's office operations, staffing (herself and Sarah full-time, Leah part-time), and budgets. Key points included: elections are the largest budget item with postage increasing from $6,000 to $15,000 for fall elections; 84% of tax collection ($22 million) is handled at the clerk's counter; pet licensing was brought back in-house saving roughly $5,500 annually compared to the third-party Pet Data service; and the quarterly newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually for postage and printing. Discussion occurred about pet licensing fee increases from $10 to $20 for neutered dogs, driven by Marathon County raising fees to cover Humane Society costs. Committee members also discussed the $175 per animal cost for non-dog strays including mice.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well for the first three Saturdays. Vendors are happy with the new setup and location. The market is full with food trucks, coffee vendors, craft people, and some produce vendors with greenhouses. Committee members raised concerns about parking on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff confirming parking is only allowed on the west side of Alderson. Mountain Bay Metro police have been monitoring the situation.

Committee member Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for his time serving. The chair noted the committee now has open spots and encouraged members to reach out to interested citizens.

actionItems: CDA to pass resolution to call TIF bonds, anticipated this summer before October 1st deadline
Staff to contact Ehlers to write letter to US Bank Associate Trust for bond payoff
Seek candidates to fill committee vacancies
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to Chapter 50 nuisances, recommended an e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions, and authorized razing of an abandoned structure. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations and a crosswalk request, deferring both items for further staff work.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Department reports - Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police
E-bicycle ordinance in Chapter 82 traffic and vehicles
Overnight parking regulations October 31 to May 1
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment

discussions.body: Jim Senalt addressed the committee with multiple concerns about the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, stating it was 'a terrible execution' and predicting the grassy areas would be destroyed by summer. He questioned ADA-compliant parking placement on grass rather than asphalt, asked about the origin of new fire inspections for food trucks, and expressed concern about accountability on longstanding code enforcement cases, noting some have been pending for years with no visible progress.

Code Enforcement reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the enforcement roll after the property sale and new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt noted the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week and others starting in May and January. Two SROs work summer zoning violations for all three municipalities and handle pet licensing compliance. Reports were acknowledged by motion from Sherwanka, seconded by Mosen, passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including tree limb height requirements over streets and walkways (switched to 14 and 10 feet as discussed previously) with language about complying with jurisdiction authority. Motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees made by Olsen, seconded by Kimi, passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion occurred regarding emotos (electric mini-motorcycles). Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting emotos on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes and citing safety concerns including complaints about people doing wheelies. Committee member Mike raised questions about electric scooters used by middle schoolers. The committee decided to add language prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property. Motion by Olsen to recommend approval with emoto restrictions, seconded by Sherwanka, passed unanimously. Rothschild has not yet approved the ordinance; Scofield indicated they didn't feel it was necessary.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour no-parking window (4am-4pm) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities, which typically use 5-hour windows overnight. Chief Hunt opposed eliminating overnight parking, noting it gives officers time to clear cars before plows arrive. DPW Director Michael Wodalski suggested signing 6-8 specific problem areas rather than village-wide restrictions, noting most residential plowing doesn't begin until 7-8am. The committee deferred action, directing Michael to identify specific exception areas and return in July with recommendations.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between their property and DC Everest school parking lot they use on Sundays. Staff estimated a basic crosswalk would cost approximately $2,000; a lighted system would be $25,000-$30,000. Committee members were reluctant to set precedent of paying for crosswalks on request. Motion by Olsen that if Mount Olive agrees to cover costs, the committee recommends board approval; seconded by Sherwanka, passed unanimously. Michael will contact Mount Olive about cost-sharing.

Chief Hunt explained a regional fire inspection program involving Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Safer where any participating department can inspect food trucks with a sticker valid for all municipalities. Fire inspector Marty already inspected one truck that failed due to galvanized steel LP piping and improperly secured propane containers. Wausau opted out to do their own inspections. Motion by Sherwanka to approve Ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: 5915 Delicoski Street needs split to 5915/5917 as it's a duplex; Ross Avenue addresses need resequencing due to an existing apartment building addressed as 3909; and 2211 Radkey Avenue needs correction for a new duplex on a private drive. Chief Hunt also identified Weston Villa Apartments on Delicowski as having eight separate addresses for one structure. Motion passed unanimously to recommend address corrections to the board.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings on an abandoned structure. The property has been abandoned since 2010-2011, posted for years, and was previously considered for a fire department training burn but was deemed too unsafe. Staff discovered the owners had been housing chickens inside during winter. The current owners are up to date on taxes and preferred the raze approach. Demolition costs will be placed on the tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke during public comment, criticizing the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park as 'a terrible execution' that would destroy grassy areas. He raised concerns about ADA parking being placed on grass rather than asphalt, questioned the need for new fire inspections on food trucks, noted ambiguity in food truck regulations for public property, and criticized the lack of accountability on longstanding code enforcement cases.
actionItems: Chapter 50 nuisance amendments recommended for board approval with tree limb height corrections
E-bicycle ordinance recommended for board approval with emotos prohibited on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property
Michael Wodalski to identify 6-8 specific areas needing overnight parking restrictions and return in July
Michael Wodalski to contact Mount Olive Church about paying for crosswalk installation
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections approved for board consideration
Address corrections for E911 compliance recommended for board approval
Raze order authorized for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster in yard
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
